mnemonics.title: A PIED PIP
mnemonics.content: A PIED PIP - Address of (*), Pointer, Indirection (*), Exclamation (!), Data Types, Parentheses (), Increment/Decrement (++/--), Pointer Arithmetic. This phrase helps recall all pointer operators and concepts in order of precedence and usage.

mnemonics.title: M.C. FR
mnemonics.content: M.C. FR - Malloc, Calloc, Free, Realloc. The sequence of memory functions used in dynamic memory: first allocate with malloc or calloc, use the memory, then free it. Realloc adjusts size if needed.

mnemonics.title: Mr. FREE
mnemonics.content: Mr. FREE - Memory must be Returned, E very E nd. A reminder that every dynamically allocated memory MUST be explicitly freed to prevent memory leaks.

mnemonics.title: DANGLING
mnemonics.content: DANGLING - Don't Access Null, Gone, Lost, Invalid, Null, Garbage. A checklist for avoiding dangling pointers: accessing NULL, using freed memory, lost pointer, invalid memory, uninitialized, or garbage values.

mnemonics.title: STAR Rule
mnemonics.content: STAR - Store The Address in Register. A pointer stores the address (register) of another variable. In declaration: int *ptr means ptr is a pointer to int. In expression: *ptr means access the value at that address.

acronyms.usage: Allocates a single block of specified bytes. Returns void* (generic pointer). Example: ptr = (int*)malloc(10 * sizeof(int));
Allocates multiple blocks and initializes to zero. Takes two arguments: number of elements and size of each element. Example: ptr = (int*)calloc(10, sizeof(int));
Resizes previously allocated memory block. Can increase or decrease size. Returns new pointer (may be different from original). Example: ptr = (int*)realloc(ptr, 20 * sizeof(int));
Releases dynamically allocated memory. Must be called for every malloc/calloc/realloc to prevent memory leaks. Example: free(ptr);

acronyms.expansion: No Utility or Logical Location / Not a Valid Address
acronyms.usage: Represents an invalid or uninitialized pointer. Always check for NULL after malloc/calloc/realloc before using the pointer.
Memory allocated at runtime using heap section. Size can be determined during program execution, unlike static/stack memory.
Returns size in bytes of data type or variable. Essential for portable code. Use with pointers: sizeof(char), sizeof(int), sizeof(variable).

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a pointer variable as an arrow. The arrow starts from the pointer variable and points to the target variable. The value inside the pointer is the address (house number), and *pointer gives you what's inside the house.
Think of Stack as a stack of plates - items are added/removed from top only (LIFO). Heap is like a vacant playground - you can grab any available space, it's scattered and unorganized.
Visualize a warehouse (heap) with numbered storage boxes. malloc is like reserving one specific-sized box. The pointer is your keycard that knows which box number (address) you reserved.

Think of free as returning your hotel room key. The room (memory) is now available for others, but keep the keycard (pointer) - it still holds the old room number (dangling pointer). Set pointer to NULL after freeing.

When you add 1 to an int pointer, it jumps by 4 bytes (sizeof int). Imagine pointer as a ruler marked in int-sized steps, not individual bytes. Each increment moves to the next complete integer location.
A NULL pointer is like a map with 'You Are Here' pointing to nowhere - it's a valid pointer variable but it doesn't point to any valid memory location yet.

analogies.analog: A pointer is like a house address written on a piece of paper. The address-of operator (&) is like asking 'What is your home address?' and writing it down. The pointer stores that address.
Using *pointer is like using the address to actually go to that house and knock on the door to see who is inside. You access the value at that location.
malloc is like finding one empty parking spot and taking it (random values in memory). calloc is like finding a row of consecutive spots and cleaning them (initializes to zero) before parking.
Memory leak is like renting a hotel room and never checking out. You keep renting new rooms but never return the old ones. Eventually, the hotel (system memory) runs out of rooms for others.
A dangling pointer is like keeping an old address card to a house that was demolished. The address still exists on your card but the house is gone - accessing it causes problems.
A dynamic array is like a shopping list that expands as you add items. Unlike a fixed notepad (static array), you can request more space using realloc when needed.
